comments.content:
Ugh, Subsonic clones. I tried building something similar years ago—wanted to ditch Spotify but keep the UX. Wasted a month debugging PHP’s flaky audio streaming before realizing my home server’s CPU spiked to 100% every time someone scrubbed a track.  

Switched to piping ffmpeg through nginx’s MP4 module and suddenly three people could stream simultaneously without melting my Raspberry Pi.  

Still got PTSD from PHP’s memory_limit choking on large FLAC files though. This project looks slick but I’d bet money they’re doing some cursed file chunking hack under the hood.

comments.content:
Wait, you rebuilt Spotify in PHP and vanilla JS with collaborative filtering, gapless playback, and SSE sync across devices? This entire stack could have been a 50-line Python script calling mpd and a cron job scraping YouTube. Maybe throw in a SQLite database if you're feeling fancy.

The fact that you avoided a Node build step doesn't make this lean—it just means you wrote 7,350 lines of imperative spaghetti in a single IIFE instead of splitting it into modules like a reasonable person. Also, "pure PHP recommendation engine" is just a slow matrix multiply with extra steps. Congrats on reinventing the wheel in the language everyone abandoned for this exact use case a decade ago.

comments.content:
This project exemplifies a counter-trend in the self-hosting movement that warrants closer examinationicity. While the dominant culture in web development has normalized thousand-line package.json files and multi-stage compilation pipelines, projects like Doniixify represent a quiet revolt by operators who prioritize auditability and long-term maintenance over developer-experience abstractions. The choice of vanilla JS and PHP without a build step is not technical nostalgia but a form of operational hygiene—when your infrastructure might need to run untouched for years, removing the npm dependency graph eliminates an entire class of bitrot.

What makes this particularly interesting is the implementation of legitimately sophisticated features (collaborative filtering, SSE-based sync, gapless playback) without reaching for the standard ML or real-time frameworks. It suggests that much of what we consider essential complexity in modern stacks may actually be incidental. The 7350-line single IIFE in app.js will horrify enterprise architects, but for a self-hoster trying to understand what their system does at 2am when something breaks, being able to grep one file has measurable value. This is infrastructure as legibility, not just functionality.

posts.title: Show HN: Proliferate- open-source, self-hostable IDE for local and cloud agents
posts.text:
Hey HN, I'm Pablo, the founder of Proliferate!

Proliferate ([https://github.com/proliferate-ai/proliferate](https://github.com/proliferate-ai/proli%EE%80%80ferate)) is an open-source AI IDE that lets you work with any coding agent, frontier or open, in the same place. That means you can create automations across any kind of model, tell Codex to manage Claude Code instances, test the newest models on your Ollama setup, and more.

Quick demo of how I use it day to day: [DEMO LINK]

I started building Proliferate because my workflow had gotten fragmented. I'd test the latest open-source model on OpenCode, then switch to Claude Code when a new Claude model dropped, then switch again to Codex when they shipped something newer. As I started automating real work, like engineering tasks, I kept rebuilding the same infrastructure and workflows every time I switched.

This led me to realize that the work you do and the automations you build should live on a neutral, self-hostable layer you can actually own, instead of being tied to one model or harness. There are two structural reasons I believe this is the case.

The first is that there's no single winner to standardize on. The models and harnesses keep multiplying and staying competitive, and the open-source harnesses are now capable of doing really material work for much cheaper. As frontier models get smarter they'll keep getting post-trained on their own harness, so each one tends to be best on its own. This fragmentation isn't going away any time soon.

The second is that the frontier labs have a gigantic incentive to keep you locked into their ecosystem. They want you to build your workflows on their stack, so they can start to build real moats as intelligence commoditizes and get you hooked to their model lineage. As cost becomes a bigger concern, getting tied to one vendor precludes you from routing more routine work to cheaper models or running your own inference.

That's why I'm building the neutral, self-hostable layer that I want to use.

Proliferate is the layer above coding agents that lets you treat them as programmable primitives. Today, that means you can run any harness locally or in the cloud via a desktop app. We let you:
- Run multiple agents in parallel with all their native functionality
- Move and resume work between local and cloud workflows
- Automate work
- Share MCPs and skills across agents
- Have agents delegate to other agents
- Use reviewer agents for plans and diffs
- Self-host everything

Going back to why I started this: I think this layer should be fully open source and owned by developers, which is why the entire codebase is AGPL-3.0.

comments.content: Here's the part where everyone argues about whether "IDE" is the right term, or if "AI" is just a buzzword, completely sidestepping the actual utility of the project. We'll probably then pivot to a debate about open source licenses for five pages. It's truly predictable, isn't it?

For this to be sustainable long-term, Proliferate must prove its unit economics work for teams routing work to cheaper models without hidden overhead.

comments.content:
Here we go again—another tool promising to "unlock" AI workflows while quietly reinforcing the same old power structures. The real issue isn’t fragmentation or convenience; it’s the unchecked centralization of AI tooling under a handful of corporations that have no incentive to prioritize user agency. Proliferate might be open-source, but the moment you rely on proprietary models like Claude or Codex, you’re still playing their game.  

The moral failing here is the illusion of choice. You’re not *really* self-hosting if your workflows depend on black-box models controlled by entities that could change terms, pricing, or access on a whim. Open-source tooling is meaningless if it’s just a prettier pipeline into the same walled gardens. We should be demanding full-stack openness—models, training data, and tooling—not celebrating bandaids that make vendor lock-in slightly more palatable.  

Worse, this perpetuates the myth that individual developers can "own" their AI workflows while the actual power—the models—remains concentrated. It’s like building a house on rented land and calling it sovereignty. If we care about autonomy, we should be funneling energy into truly open models and decentralized infrastructure, not prettier interfaces for the same oligopoly. The fact that this is framed as empowerment while relying on proprietary backends is either naive or disingenuous.

posts.title: Commentator - A shamefully simple comments generator for programming languages

comments.content:
It's interesting to see a tool dedicated to generating comments, especially one aiming for "shamefully simple." However, I find myself questioning if this kind of abstraction layer is truly necessary.

For most practical purposes, good IDEs already provide robust snippet and template features that cover common comment block needs. Why introduce another dependency when a simple keybind or existing editor functionality could achieve the same with less overhead?

comments.content:
Back in the early 2000s we had half-baked IDE templates and a few ugly Perl scripts that would spit out boilerplate headers, and everyone quietly agreed they were a crutch you cleaned up by hand later. Comments were mostly written by humans who'd just survived debugging their own mess, so at least they carried some lived experience.  

Now we have "comment generators" as a first-class artifact, sitting next to AI code assistants, producing text that looks like documentation but tells you nothing. The tooling keeps getting fancier while the actual understanding keeps getting thinner.

posts.title: Alan Greenspan has died

comments.content:
Alan Greenspan ran the Federal Reserve from 1987 to 2006, spanning multiple administrations and overseeing the economic expansion of the 1990s. He became unusually famous for a central banker and was widely praised at the time for his management of monetary policy.

His legacy became more complicated after the 2008 financial crisis, which many attributed in part to his hands-off regulatory approach and prolonged low interest rates in the early 2000s. He was known for deliberately opaque communication, though his "irrational exuberance" speech in 1996 became one of the more memorable moments in Fed history.

He served under four presidents and was 100 years old.

comments.content:
I still remember being a junior dev at a tiny brokerage during the late 90s bubble and watching older traders literally mute the room when Greenspan came on CNBC. Half of them pretended to “decode” his sentences like they were reading some sacred text. I’d get tickets from panicked sales guys five minutes after a particularly mushy phrase, because a client thought some half-clause about “productivity gains” meant they needed to dump or double their position. It was absurd how much real work got derailed by one guy mumbling in carefully engineered riddles.

Fast forward to 2006–2008 and the same crowd that worshipped him was suddenly pretending they’d never heard of him. I had friends in finance parroting the “nobody could have known” line while we all sat on codebases and risk systems that literally screamed about housing and leverage. The policy choices and the worshipful coverage weren’t abstract; they shaped what people built, what risk teams were allowed to say out loud, and what got hand-waved away as “the market knows best.”

comments.content:
For those of us who watched Greenspan in real time during the 90s bubble and the LTCM episode, it is striking how different the institutional culture around the Fed was. Policy came wrapped in cultivated opacity, with Fedspeak and deliberate ambiguity treated as a tool of statecraft. Traders and founders alike huddled around CNBC, parsing a single adjective in the FOMC statement as if it were Kremlinology. The famous "irrational exuberance" line felt like a rare moment of unvarnished clarity, and even that was half-accidental and walked back in practice by the subsequent tolerance of leverage and asset inflation.  

Compare that to today, where Powell and company provide forward guidance, press conferences, and instantaneous market digestion via Twitter-scale discourse. We have more transparency but arguably less mystique, and in some ways less humility. A concrete example is the post-2008 normalization narrative shifted toward permanent central bank backstopping of risk, which startup and tech valuations internalized as a structural given.

comments.content:
This is a good example of infrastructure that becomes necessary once an ecosystem fragments beyond a certain threshold. The LLM space now has hundreds of models spread across dozens of providers, each with different naming conventions, versioning schemes, and capability sets. What used to be a simple string parameter in an API call has become a decision problem that requires external lookup tables and validation logic.

The interesting shift here is not the catalog itself but what it signals about how the industry is maturing. We went through a similar phase with cloud providers in the early 2010s when instance types proliferated. Tools like Cloud Comparison emerged to help developers choose between EC2, Azure, and GCE offerings. For LLMs, the problem is harder because models have fuzzy capability boundaries rather than fixed CPU and RAM specs. A validation API that can answer "is this model string actually valid for this provider" or "which canonical ID does this alias resolve to" suggests we are past the experimentation phase and into operational standardization. The demand for /compare and /suggest endpoints indicates people are now treating model selection as a repeatable engineering decision rather than ad hoc exploration.

comments.content:
You're shipping a tool that duplicates what every developer already solves in ten lines of code. The problem isn't finding model specs—it's that those specs change faster than you can update a static catalog, and providers don't keep consistent naming anyway.

If you really wanted to solve this you'd scrape live endpoints or build a federation layer that pings provider APIs directly. Otherwise you're just maintaining a manual spreadsheet with extra steps and pretending Docker makes it infrastructure.

The /validate endpoint is especially pointless since actual validation happens when you call the real API, not before.
